title: Hermann Göring

infobox.value: * [Franz von Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen") (1932) * [Kurt von Schleicher](/wiki/Kurt_von_Schleicher "Kurt von Schleicher") (1932–1933) * Adolf Hitler (1933–1945)

*Office abolished*   * [Erich Köhler](/wiki/Erich_K%C3%B6hler "Erich Köhler") ([President of the West German Bundestag](/wiki/President_of_the_Bundestag "President of the Bundestag") in 1949) * [Johannes Dieckmann](/wiki/Johannes_Dieckmann "Johannes Dieckmann") ([President of the East German People's Chamber](/wiki/Leadership_of_East_Germany#Heads_of_parliament "Leadership of East Germany") in 1949)
[Carin von Kantzow](/wiki/Carin_G%C3%B6ring "Carin Göring")  ​  ​ (m. 1923; died 1931)​      [Emmy Sonnemann](/wiki/Emmy_G%C3%B6ring "Emmy Göring")  ​ (m. 1935)​

* *[Reichsmarschall](/wiki/Reichsmarschall "Reichsmarschall")* * *[SA](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung")-[Obergruppenführer](/wiki/Obergruppenf%C3%BChrer "Obergruppenführer")* * *[Reichsforst-](/wiki/Ranks_and_insignia_of_the_Forest_protection_(Nazi_Germany) "Ranks and insignia of the Forest protection (Nazi Germany)") und [Reichsjägermeister](/wiki/Deutsche_J%C3%A4gerschaft "Deutsche Jägerschaft")*
* [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") * [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")
* *[Pour le Mérite](/wiki/Pour_le_M%C3%A9rite "Pour le Mérite")* * [Grand Cross of the Iron Cross](/wiki/Grand_Cross_of_the_Iron_Cross "Grand Cross of the Iron Cross")

[Conspiracy to commit crimes against peace](/wiki/Crimes_of_aggression "Crimes of aggression") [Crimes of aggression](/wiki/Crimes_of_aggression "Crimes of aggression") [War crimes](/wiki/War_crimes "War crimes") [Crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity")

(or **Goering**;[[a]](#cite_note-spelling-4) German: [[ˈhɛʁman ˈvɪlhɛlm ˈɡøːʁɪŋ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-HermannWGoering.ogg "File:De-HermannWGoering.ogg"); 12 January 1893 – 15 October 1946) was a German politician, aviator, military leader, and convicted war criminal. He was one of the most powerful figures in the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"), which controlled Germany from 1933 to 1945. He also served as *[Oberbefehlshaber der Luftwaffe](/wiki/Oberkommando_der_Luftwaffe "Oberkommando der Luftwaffe")* (Supreme Commander of the Air Force), a position he held until the final days of the regime.

**Hermann Wilhelm Göring** (or **Goering**;[[a]](#cite_note-spelling-4) German: [[ˈhɛʁman ˈvɪlhɛlm ˈɡøːʁɪŋ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-HermannWGoering.ogg "File:De-HermannWGoering.ogg"); 12 January 1893 – 15 October 1946) was a German politician, aviator, military leader, and convicted war criminal. He was one of the most powerful figures in the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"), which controlled Germany from 1933 to 1945. He also served as *[Oberbefehlshaber der Luftwaffe](/wiki/Oberkommando_der_Luftwaffe "Oberkommando der Luftwaffe")* (Supreme Commander of the Air Force), a position he held until the final days of the regime.

He was born in [Rosenheim](/wiki/Rosenheim "Rosenheim"), [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria"). A veteran [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") fighter pilot [ace](/wiki/Flying_aces "Flying aces"), Göring was a recipient of the *[Pour le Mérite](/wiki/Pour_le_M%C3%A9rite "Pour le Mérite")*. He served as the last commander of [*Jagdgeschwader* 1](/wiki/Jagdgeschwader_1_(World_War_I) "Jagdgeschwader 1 (World War I)") (JG I), the fighter wing once led by [Manfred von Richthofen](/wiki/Manfred_von_Richthofen "Manfred von Richthofen"). An early member of the Nazi Party, Göring was among those wounded in [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")'s failed [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch") in 1923. While receiving treatment for his injuries, he developed an addiction to [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") that persisted until the last year of his life. After Hitler became [Chancellor of Germany](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") in 1933, Göring was named as [minister without portfolio](/wiki/Minister_without_portfolio "Minister without portfolio") in the new government. One of his first acts as a cabinet minister was to oversee the creation of the [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo"), which he ceded to [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") in 1934.

Following the establishment of the Nazi state, Göring amassed power and political capital to become the second most powerful man in Germany. Upon being named [Plenipotentiary](/wiki/Plenipotentiary "Plenipotentiary") of the [Four Year Plan](/wiki/Four_Year_Plan "Four Year Plan") in 1936, Göring was entrusted with the task of mobilising all sectors of the economy for war, an assignment which brought numerous government agencies under his control. In September 1939, Hitler gave a speech to the *Reichstag* designating him as his successor. After the [Fall of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France") in 1940, he was bestowed the specially created rank of *[Reichsmarschall](/wiki/Reichsmarschall "Reichsmarschall")*, which gave him seniority over all officers in [Germany's armed forces](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht").

By 1941, Göring was at the peak of his power and influence. As the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") progressed, Göring's standing with Hitler and the German public declined after the Luftwaffe proved incapable of [preventing the Allied bombing of Germany's cities](/wiki/Defence_of_the_Reich "Defence of the Reich") and [resupplying surrounded Axis forces in Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad#Sixth_Army_surrounded "Battle of Stalingrad"). Around that time, Göring increasingly withdrew from military and political affairs to devote his attention to collecting property and artwork, much of which was stolen from Jewish victims of [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"). Informed on 22 April 1945 that Hitler intended to commit suicide, Göring sent [a telegram to Hitler](/wiki/G%C3%B6ring_telegram "Göring telegram") requesting his permission to assume leadership of the Reich. Considering his request an act of treason, Hitler removed Göring from all his positions, expelled him from the party and ordered his arrest.

After the war, Göring was convicted of [conspiracy](/wiki/Conspiracy "Conspiracy"), [crimes against peace](/wiki/Crimes_against_peace "Crimes against peace"), [war crimes](/wiki/War_crime "War crime"), and [crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity") at the [Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials") in 1946. He requested at trial an execution by firing squad, but was denied; instead he was sentenced to death by hanging. He died of suicide by [ingesting cyanide](/wiki/Cyanide_poisoning "Cyanide poisoning") the night before his scheduled execution.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R25668%2C_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R25668%2C_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R25668,_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring.jpg)

Göring in 1907, at age 14

Hermann Wilhelm Göring was born on 12 January 1893[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201121-5) at the Marienbad Sanatorium in [Rosenheim](/wiki/Rosenheim "Rosenheim"), [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria"). His father, [Heinrich Ernst Göring](/wiki/Heinrich_Ernst_G%C3%B6ring "Heinrich Ernst Göring") (31 October 1839 – 7 December 1913), a former cavalry officer, had been the first [governor-general of German South West Africa](/wiki/List_of_colonial_governors_of_South_West_Africa "List of colonial governors of South West Africa") (modern-day [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia")).[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlockTrow1971327–330-6) Heinrich had three children from a previous marriage. Göring was the fourth of five children by Heinrich's second wife, Franziska Tiefenbrunn (1859–15 July 1943), a Bavarian peasant. Göring's elder siblings were Karl, Olga, and Paula; his younger brother was [Albert](/wiki/Albert_G%C3%B6ring "Albert Göring"). At the time that Göring was born, his father was serving as consul general in [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"),[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery20125-7) and his mother had returned home briefly to give birth. She left the six-week-old baby with a friend in Bavaria and did not see the child again for three years, when she and Heinrich returned to Germany.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201121–22-8)

Göring's godfather was [Hermann Epenstein](/wiki/Hermann_Epenstein "Hermann Epenstein"), a wealthy Jewish physician and businessman his father had met in Africa. Epenstein provided the Göring family, who were surviving on Heinrich's pension, first with a family home in Berlin-Friedenau,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFreitag201525–45-9) and then a small castle called [Burg Veldenstein](/w/index.php?title=Burg_Veldenstein&action=edit&redlink=1 "Burg Veldenstein (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Burg_Veldenstein "de:Burg Veldenstein")], near [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"). Göring's mother became Epenstein's mistress around this time and remained so for some fifteen years. Epenstein acquired the minor title of [Ritter](/wiki/Ritter "Ritter") (knight) von Epenstein through service and donations to the Crown.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201122–24-10)

Interested in a career as a soldier from a very early age, Göring enjoyed playing with toy soldiers and dressing up in a [Boer](/wiki/Boer "Boer") uniform his father had given him. He was sent to boarding school at age eleven, where the food was poor and discipline harsh. He sold a violin to pay for his train ticket home and then took to his bed, feigning illness, until he was told he would not have to return.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201124–25-11) He continued to enjoy war games, pretending to lay siege to the castle Veldenstein and studying [Teutonic](/wiki/Teutons "Teutons") legends and sagas. He became a mountain climber, scaling peaks in Germany, at the [Mont Blanc massif](/wiki/Mont_Blanc_massif "Mont Blanc massif") and in the [Austrian Alps](/wiki/Austrian_Alps "Austrian Alps"). At age 16, he was sent to a military academy in [Berlin-Lichterfelde](/wiki/Lichterfelde_(Berlin) "Lichterfelde (Berlin)"), from which he graduated with distinction.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201124–28-12)

Göring joined the Prince Wilhelm Regiment (112th Infantry, Garrison: [Mülhausen](/wiki/Mulhouse "Mulhouse")) of the [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army") in 1912. The next year his mother had a falling-out with Epenstein. The family was forced to leave Veldenstein and moved to [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"); Göring's father died shortly afterwards. It was in Bavaria where Göring developed his "romantic sense of Germanness" that further evolved under Nazism.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery20125-7) When World War I began in August 1914, Göring was stationed at [Mülhausen](/wiki/M%C3%BClhausen "Mülhausen") with his regiment.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201124–28-12)

## World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: World War I")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Goring_WW1.gif/250px-Goring_WW1.gif)](/wiki/File:Goring_WW1.gif)

Film clip of Göring in a [Fokker D.VII](/wiki/Fokker_D.VII "Fokker D.VII") during World War I (1918)

During the first year of World War I, Göring served with his infantry regiment in the area of [Mülhausen](/wiki/M%C3%BClhausen "Mülhausen"), a garrison town less than 2 km from the French frontier. He was hospitalised with [rheumatism](/wiki/Rheumatism "Rheumatism"), a result of the damp of [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare"). While he was recovering, his friend [Bruno Loerzer](/wiki/Bruno_Loerzer "Bruno Loerzer") convinced him to transfer to what would become, by October 1916, the *[Luftstreitkräfte](/wiki/Luftstreitkr%C3%A4fte "Luftstreitkräfte")* (transl. air combat forces) of the German army, but his request was turned down. Later that year, Göring flew as Loerzer's observer in *[Feldflieger Abteilung](/wiki/Feldflieger_Abteilung "Feldflieger Abteilung")* 25 (FFA 25); Göring had informally transferred himself. He was discovered and sentenced to three weeks' confinement to barracks, but the sentence was never carried out. By the time it was supposed to be imposed, Göring's association with Loerzer had been made official. They were assigned as a team to FFA 25 in the [Crown Prince](/wiki/William,_German_Crown_Prince "William, German Crown Prince")'s Fifth Army. They flew reconnaissance and bombing missions, for which the crown prince invested both Göring and Loerzer with the [Iron Cross](/wiki/Iron_Cross "Iron Cross"), first class.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201128–29-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Nicola_Perscheid_-_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_um_1918.jpg/250px-Nicola_Perscheid_-_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_um_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicola_Perscheid_-_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_um_1918.jpg)

Göring with the *[Pour le Mérite](/wiki/Pour_le_M%C3%A9rite "Pour le Mérite")* and several awards

After completing the pilot's training course, Göring was assigned to [*Jagdstaffel* 5](/wiki/Jagdstaffel_5 "Jagdstaffel 5"). Seriously wounded in the hip in aerial combat, he took nearly a year to recover. He then was transferred to [*Jagdstaffel* 26](/wiki/Jagdstaffel_26 "Jagdstaffel 26"), commanded by Loerzer, in February 1917. He steadily scored [air victories](/wiki/Dogfight "Dogfight") until May, when he was assigned to command [*Jagdstaffel* 27](/wiki/Jagdstaffel_27 "Jagdstaffel 27"). Serving with *Jastas* 5, 26 and 27, he continued to win victories. In addition to his Iron Crosses (1st and 2nd Class), he received the [Zähringer Lion](/wiki/Order_of_the_Z%C3%A4hringer_Lion "Order of the Zähringer Lion") with swords, the [Friedrich Order](/wiki/Friedrich_Order "Friedrich Order"), the [House Order of Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_Order_of_Hohenzollern "House Order of Hohenzollern") with swords third class and, finally, in May 1918, the coveted *[Pour le Mérite](/wiki/Pour_le_M%C3%A9rite "Pour le Mérite")*.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201131–32-14) According to [Hermann Dahlmann](/wiki/Hermann_Dahlmann "Hermann Dahlmann"), who knew both men, Göring had Loerzer lobby for the award.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranksBaileyGuest199395,_117,_156-15) He finished the war with [22 victories](/wiki/List_of_World_War_I_aces_credited_with_20_or_more_victories "List of World War I aces credited with 20 or more victories").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranksBaileyGuest1993117-16) A thorough post-war examination of [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") loss records showed that only two of his awarded victories were doubtful. Three were possible and 17 were certain, or highly likely.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKilduff2013165–166-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Herman_Goering_1918_Jasta_11.jpg/250px-Herman_Goering_1918_Jasta_11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Herman_Goering_1918_Jasta_11.jpg)

Göring in 1918 as commander of [*Jagdgeschwader* 1](/wiki/Jagdgeschwader_1_(World_War_I) "Jagdgeschwader 1 (World War I)") beside his [Fokker D VII](/wiki/Fokker_D_VII "Fokker D VII") (F) 5125/18. He holds a walking stick *Geschwader Stock* that had been owned by [Manfred von Richthofen](/wiki/Manfred_von_Richthofen "Manfred von Richthofen").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranksVan_Wyngarden200315,_17,_92-18)

On 7 July 1918, following the death of [Wilhelm Reinhard](/wiki/Wilhelm_Reinhard_(pilot) "Wilhelm Reinhard (pilot)"), successor to [Manfred von Richthofen](/wiki/Manfred_von_Richthofen "Manfred von Richthofen"), Göring was made commander of the "Flying Circus", [*Jagdgeschwader* 1](/wiki/Jagdgeschwader_1_(World_War_I) "Jagdgeschwader 1 (World War I)").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201131–33-19) His arrogance made him unpopular with the men of his squadron.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011403-20) In one incident, pilot [Willi Gabriel](/wiki/Willi_Gabriel "Willi Gabriel") took off against Göring's orders and shot down several enemy planes. When he returned to base, Göring ordered him grounded.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranksBaileyGuest1993112–113-21)[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

In the last days of the war, Göring was repeatedly ordered to withdraw his squadron, first to [Tellancourt](/wiki/Tellancourt "Tellancourt") airdrome, then to [Darmstadt](/wiki/Darmstadt "Darmstadt"). At one point, he was ordered to surrender the aircraft to the Allies; he refused. Many of his pilots intentionally crash-landed their planes to keep them from falling into enemy hands.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201134–36-22)

Like many other German veterans, Göring was a proponent of the [stab-in-the-back myth](/wiki/Stab-in-the-back_myth "Stab-in-the-back myth"), the belief which held that the German Army had not really lost the war, but instead was betrayed by the civilian leadership: Marxists, Jews and especially the [republicans](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), who had overthrown the German monarchy.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201139-23) Atop the frustration of military defeat, Göring also experienced the personal disappointment of being snubbed by his fiancée's upper-class family, who broke off the engagement when he returned penniless from the front.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery20125–6-24)

## After World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: After World War I")]

Göring remained in aviation after the war. He tried [barnstorming](/wiki/Barnstorming "Barnstorming") and briefly worked at [Fokker](/wiki/Fokker "Fokker"). After spending most of 1919 living in [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), he moved to Sweden and joined *[Svensk Lufttrafik](/wiki/Svensk_Lufttrafik "Svensk Lufttrafik")*, a Swedish airline. Göring was often hired for private flights. During the winter of 1920–1921, he was hired by [Count Eric von Rosen](/wiki/Count_Eric_von_Rosen "Count Eric von Rosen") to fly him to his castle from Stockholm. Invited to spend the night, Göring may at this time have first seen the [swastika](/wiki/Swastika "Swastika") emblem, which Rosen had set in the chimney piece as a family badge.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201139–41-25)[[b]](#cite_note-swastika-26)

This was also the first time that Göring saw his future wife; the count introduced his sister-in-law, Baroness [Carin von Kantzow](/wiki/Carin_G%C3%B6ring "Carin Göring") ([née](/wiki/Birth_name#Maiden_and_married_names "Birth name") Freiin von Fock). Estranged from her husband of 10 years, she had an eight-year-old son. Göring was immediately infatuated and asked her to meet him in Stockholm. They arranged a visit at the home of her parents and spent much time together through 1921, when Göring left to study political science at the [University of Munich](/wiki/University_of_Munich "University of Munich"). Carin obtained a divorce, followed Göring to Munich and married him on 3 February 1922.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201143-27) Their first home together was a hunting lodge at Hochkreuth in the [Bavarian Alps](/wiki/Bavarian_Alps "Bavarian Alps"), near [Bayrischzell](/wiki/Bayrischzell "Bayrischzell"), some 80 kilometres (50 mi) from Munich.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201141,_43-28) Later in 1922, they moved to [Obermenzing](/w/index.php?title=Obermenzing_(district)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Obermenzing (district) (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Obermenzing "de:Obermenzing")], a suburb of Munich.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201145,_47-29)

## Early Nazi career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early Nazi career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/76/Hitler_1929.jpg/250px-Hitler_1929.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitler_1929.jpg)

Göring (left) stands in front of [Hitler](/wiki/Hitler "Hitler") at a [Nazi Party rally](/wiki/Nuremberg_rallies "Nuremberg rallies") in [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg") (1929).

Göring joined the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") in 1922 after hearing a speech by [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201145,_47-29)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006426-30) He was given command of the *[Sturmabteilung](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung")* (SA) as the *[Oberster SA-Führer](/wiki/Oberster_SA-F%C3%BChrer "Oberster SA-Führer")* on 1 March 1923,[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008112-31) succeeding [Hans Ulrich Klintzsch](/wiki/Hans_Ulrich_Klintzsch "Hans Ulrich Klintzsch"), and headed the organisation until it was banned in November 1923.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201529,_41-32)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELepage2016140-33)

Through the early years, Carin—who liked Hitler—often played hostess to meetings of leading Nazis, including her husband as well as Hitler, [Rudolf Hess](/wiki/Rudolf_Hess "Rudolf Hess"), [Alfred Rosenberg](/wiki/Alfred_Rosenberg "Alfred Rosenberg") and [Ernst Röhm](/wiki/Ernst_R%C3%B6hm "Ernst Röhm").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201147-34) Hitler later recalled his early association with Göring:

> I liked him. I made him the head of my SA. He is the only one of its heads that ran the SA properly. I gave him a dishevelled rabble. In a very short time he had organised a division of 11,000 men.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitler1988168-35)

Hitler and the Nazi Party held mass meetings and rallies in Munich and elsewhere during the early 1920s, attempting to gain supporters in a bid for political power.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201149–51-36) Inspired by [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini")'s [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome"), the Nazis attempted to seize power on 8–9 November 1923 in a failed coup known as the [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch"). Göring, who was with Hitler leading the march to the War Ministry, was shot in the groin.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolland201154-37) Fourteen Nazis and four policemen were killed; many top Nazis, including Hitler, were arrested.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008131-38) With Carin's help, Göring was smuggled to [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck"), where he received surgery and was given morphine for the pain. He remained in hospital until 24 December.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201157–58-39) This was the beginning of his morphine addiction, which lasted until his imprisonment at Nuremberg.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1971644-40) Meanwhile, the authorities in Munich declared Göring a wanted man. The Görings—acutely short of funds and reliant on the good will of Nazi sympathisers abroad—moved from Austria to [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"). In May 1924 they visited Rome, via [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") and [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena"). Sometime in 1924, Göring met Mussolini through his contacts with members of Italy's Fascist Party;[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery20127-41) Mussolini had also expressed an interest in meeting Hitler, who was by then in prison.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201159–60-42) Hitler penned *[Mein Kampf](/wiki/Mein_Kampf "Mein Kampf")* while incarcerated, before being released in December 1924.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008160-43)

Meanwhile, personal problems continued to multiply for Göring. By 1925, Carin's mother was ill. The Görings—with difficulty—raised the money in the spring of 1925 for a journey to Sweden via Austria, [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), Poland and [Danzig](/wiki/Free_City_of_Danzig "Free City of Danzig") (now Gdańsk). Göring had become a violent morphine addict; Carin's family were shocked by his deterioration. Carin, who was ill with [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy") and a weak heart, had to allow the doctors to take charge of Göring; her son was taken by his father. Göring was certified a dangerous drug addict and was placed in [Långbro Asylum](/wiki/L%C3%A5ngbro_Asylum "Långbro Asylum") on 1 September 1925 after he had violently attacked a nurse who had refused his request for morphine.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201161-44) He was violent to the point where he had to be confined in a [straitjacket](/wiki/Straitjacket "Straitjacket"), but his psychiatrist felt he was sane; the condition was caused solely by the morphine.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011404-45) Weaned off the drug, he left the facility briefly, but had to return for further treatment. He returned to Germany when an amnesty was declared in 1927 and resumed working in the aircraft industry.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201162,_64-46) Carin Göring, ill with epilepsy and [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"),[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960146-47) died of heart failure on 17 October 1931.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2024122-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-02134%2C_Bad_Harzburg%2C_Gr%C3%BCndung_der_Harzburger_Front.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-02134%2C_Bad_Harzburg%2C_Gr%C3%BCndung_der_Harzburger_Front.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-02134,_Bad_Harzburg,_Gr%C3%BCndung_der_Harzburger_Front.jpg)

Camp service of the [NSDAP](/wiki/NSDAP "NSDAP") delegation; in the first row SS Chief [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler"), SA Chief [Ernst Röhm](/wiki/Ernst_R%C3%B6hm "Ernst Röhm") and Göring, 1931

Meanwhile, the Nazi Party was in a period of rebuilding and waiting. The economy had recovered, which meant fewer opportunities for the Nazis to agitate. The SA was reorganised, but with [Franz Pfeffer von Salomon](/wiki/Franz_Pfeffer_von_Salomon "Franz Pfeffer von Salomon") as its head rather than Göring, and the *[Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel")* (SS) was founded in 1925, initially as a bodyguard for Hitler. Membership in the party increased from 27,000 in 1925 to 108,000 in 1928 and 178,000 in 1929. In [the May 1928 elections](/wiki/1928_German_federal_election "1928 German federal election") the Nazi Party only obtained 12 seats out of an available 491 in the *[Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_(Weimar_Republic) "Reichstag (Weimar Republic)")*.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960118–121-49) Göring was elected as a representative from Bavaria.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201166-50) Having secured a seat in the *Reichstag*, Göring gained a more prominent place in the Nazi movement, since Hitler saw him as a public relations officer for Nazism in this capacity.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery20128-51) Göring continued to be elected to the Reichstag in all subsequent elections during the Weimar and Nazi regimes.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReichstag_databank-52) Electoral success also afforded Göring with access to powerful sympathisers to the Nazi cause, such as [Prince August Wilhelm of Prussia](/wiki/Prince_August_Wilhelm_of_Prussia "Prince August Wilhelm of Prussia") and the conservative-minded businessmen, [Fritz Thyssen](/wiki/Fritz_Thyssen "Fritz Thyssen") and [Hjalmar Schacht](/wiki/Hjalmar_Schacht "Hjalmar Schacht").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery20129-53) The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") led to a disastrous downturn in the German economy, and [in the 1930 election](/wiki/1930_German_federal_election "1930 German federal election"), the Nazi Party won 6,409,600 votes and 107 seats.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960136,_138-54)[[c]](#cite_note-56) In May 1931, Hitler sent Göring on a mission to the [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), where he met the future [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel201174-57) He was appointed an *SA-[Gruppenführer](/wiki/Gruppenf%C3%BChrer "Gruppenführer")* on 18 December 1931. On 1 January 1933, he was among the first to be promoted to the recently created rank of *SA-[Obergruppenführer](/wiki/Obergruppenf%C3%BChrer "Obergruppenführer")* and he held this rank on the SA rolls until 1945.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201529,_41-32)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELepage2016140-33)

In the [July 1932 election](/wiki/July_1932_German_federal_election "July 1932 German federal election"), the Nazis won 230 seats to become far and away the largest party in the *Reichstag*. By longstanding tradition, the Nazis were thus entitled to select the President of the *Reichstag*, and elected Göring to the post.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2003297-58) He would retain this position until 23 April 1945.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011315–318-59)

## Reichstag fire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reichstag fire")]

The [Reichstag fire](/wiki/Reichstag_fire "Reichstag fire") occurred on the night of 27 February 1933. Göring was one of the first to arrive on the scene. [Marinus van der Lubbe](/wiki/Marinus_van_der_Lubbe "Marinus van der Lubbe"), a [Communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist") radical, was arrested and claimed sole responsibility for the fire. Göring immediately called for a crackdown on Communists.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2003329–330-60)

The Nazis took full advantage of the fire to advance their own aims at consolidating political power. The [Reichstag Fire Decree](/wiki/Reichstag_Fire_Decree "Reichstag Fire Decree"), passed the next day on Hitler's urging, suspended basic rights and allowed state detention without [habeas corpus](/wiki/Habeas_corpus "Habeas corpus"). Activities of the [German Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Germany "Communist Party of Germany") were suppressed, and some 4,000 Party members arrested.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960194-61) Göring demanded that the prisoners should be shot, but [Rudolf Diels](/wiki/Rudolf_Diels "Rudolf Diels"), head of the Prussian political police, ignored the order.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2003331-62) Although the consensus amongst historians is that van der Lubbe set the fire,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHett2014255–259-63) a few accuse the Nazis of staging a [false flag attack](/wiki/False_flag_attack "False flag attack").[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960192-64)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBullock1999262-65) At the [Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials"), General [Franz Halder](/wiki/Franz_Halder "Franz Halder") testified that Göring admitted responsibility for starting the fire at a luncheon held on Hitler's birthday in 1942.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960193-66) In his own Nuremberg testimony, Göring denied this story.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENuremberg_Trial_Proceedings,_18_March_1946-67)

## Second marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Second marriage")]

During the early 1930s, Göring was often in the company of [Emmy Sonnemann](/wiki/Emmy_G%C3%B6ring "Emmy Göring"), an actress from [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011111-68) They were married on 10 April 1935, in Berlin. The wedding was celebrated on a huge scale. A large reception was held the night before at the [Berlin Opera House](/wiki/Berlin_State_Opera "Berlin State Opera"). Fighter aircraft flew overhead on the night of the reception and the day of the ceremony,[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011139–140-69) at which Hitler was best man.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunther194063-70) Göring's daughter, [Edda](/wiki/Edda_G%C3%B6ring "Edda Göring"), was born on 2 June 1938.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011187-71)
