When Hitler was named [chancellor of Germany](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") on 30 January 1933, Göring was appointed as *[Reichsminister](/wiki/Reichsminister "Reichsminister")* [without portfolio](/wiki/Minister_without_portfolio "Minister without portfolio") and *[Reichskommissar](/wiki/Reichskommissar "Reichskommissar")* of Aviation.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201547-72) This was followed on 11 April 1933 by his appointment as [Minister-President](/wiki/Minister-President "Minister-President") of Prussia, Prussian [interior minister](/wiki/Interior_minister "Interior minister") and chief of the Prussian police.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201550–51-73) On 25 April 1933, Hitler also delegated his powers as *[Reichsstatthalter](/wiki/Reichsstatthalter "Reichsstatthalter")* (Reich Governor) of Prussia to Göring.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008284-1) On 18 May 1933, Göring secured passage of an [enabling act](/wiki/Enabling_act "Enabling act") through the [Landtag of Prussia](/wiki/Landtag_of_Prussia "Landtag of Prussia") that conferred all legislative powers on the cabinet.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENew_York_Times,_19_May_1933-74)

Using this authority, on 8 July 1933 Göring enacted a law abolishing the [Prussian State Council](/wiki/Prussian_State_Council "Prussian State Council"), the second chamber of the Prussian legislature that represented the interests of the Prussian provinces. In its place, he created a revised non-legislative [Prussian State Council](/wiki/Prussian_State_Council_(Nazi_Germany) "Prussian State Council (Nazi Germany)") to serve merely as a body of advisors to him. Göring would serve as President of the council. It would consist, *[ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio "Ex officio")*, of the Prussian cabinet ministers and state secretaries, as well as hand-picked Nazi Party officials and other industry and society leaders selected solely by Göring.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELilla2005292–295-75) In October 1933, Göring was made a member of [Hans Frank](/wiki/Hans_Frank "Hans Frank")'s [Academy for German Law](/wiki/Academy_for_German_Law "Academy for German Law") at its inaugural meeting.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrank1933–1934253-76) In July 1934, he was appointed *Reichforstmeister*, with the rank of a *Reichsminister*, as the head of the newly created [Reich Forestry Office](/wiki/Reich_Forestry_Office "Reich Forestry Office").[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201558-77)

[Wilhelm Frick](/wiki/Wilhelm_Frick "Wilhelm Frick"), the Reich interior minister, and the head of the SS, [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler"), hoped to create a unified police force for all of Germany, but Göring on 26 April 1933 established a special Prussian police force, with [Rudolf Diels](/wiki/Rudolf_Diels "Rudolf Diels") at its head. The force was called the *Geheime Staatspolizei* (transl. Secret State Police), or [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo"). Göring, thinking that Diels was not ruthless enough to use the Gestapo effectively to counteract the power of the SA, handed over control of the Gestapo to Himmler on 20 April 1934.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans200554-78) By this time, the SA numbered over two million men.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldhagen199695-79)

Hitler was deeply concerned that [Ernst Röhm](/wiki/Ernst_R%C3%B6hm "Ernst Röhm"), the chief of the SA, was planning a coup. Himmler and [Reinhard Heydrich](/wiki/Reinhard_Heydrich "Reinhard Heydrich") plotted with Göring to use the Gestapo and SS to crush the SA.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008306-80) Members of the SA got wind of the proposed action and thousands of them took to the streets in violent demonstrations on the night of 29 June 1934. Enraged, Hitler ordered the arrest of the SA leadership. Röhm was shot dead in his cell when he refused to commit suicide; Göring personally went over the lists of prisoners—numbering in the thousands—and determined who else should be shot. At least 85 people were killed in the period of 30 June to 2 July, which is now known as the [Night of the Long Knives](/wiki/Night_of_the_Long_Knives "Night of the Long Knives").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans200531–35,_39-81) Hitler admitted in the Reichstag on 13 July that the killings had been entirely illegal but claimed a plot had been under way to overthrow the Reich. A retroactive law was passed making the action legal. Any criticism was met with arrests.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans200538-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-C01410%2C_Berlin%2C_G%C3%B6ring_auf_der_%22Gr%C3%BCnen_Woche%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-C01410%2C_Berlin%2C_G%C3%B6ring_auf_der_%22Gr%C3%BCnen_Woche%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-C01410,_Berlin,_G%C3%B6ring_auf_der_%22Gr%C3%BCnen_Woche%22.jpg)

Göring attending the [Green Week](/wiki/Berlin_International_Green_Week "Berlin International Green Week") in Berlin, 1937

One of the terms of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles"), which had been in place since the end of World War I, stated that Germany was not allowed to maintain an air force. After the 1928 signing of the [Kellogg–Briand Pact](/wiki/Kellogg%E2%80%93Briand_Pact "Kellogg–Briand Pact"), police aircraft were permitted. Göring was appointed Air Traffic Minister in May 1933. Germany began to accumulate aircraft in violation of the Treaty, and in 1935 the existence of the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") was formally acknowledged,[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011116–117-83) with Göring as Reich Aviation Minister.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005364-84)

During a cabinet meeting in September 1936, Göring and Hitler announced that the [German rearmament](/wiki/German_rearmament "German rearmament") programme must be sped up. On 18 October, Hitler named Göring as [Plenipotentiary](/wiki/Plenipotentiary "Plenipotentiary") of the Four Year Plan to undertake this task. Göring created a new organisation to administer the Plan and drew the ministries of labour and agriculture under its umbrella. He bypassed the Economics Ministry in his policy-making decisions, to the chagrin of [Hjalmar Schacht](/wiki/Hjalmar_Schacht "Hjalmar Schacht"), the minister in charge. Huge expenditures were made on rearmament, in spite of growing deficits.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005357–360-85) Schacht resigned on 26 November 1937, and Göring took over the Economics Ministry on an interim basis until January 1938.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201560-86) He then managed to install [Walther Funk](/wiki/Walther_Funk "Walther Funk") in the position, who also took control of the [Reichsbank](/wiki/Reichsbank "Reichsbank") when Schacht was forced out of that post as well in January 1939. In this way, both of these institutions effectively were brought under Göring's control under the auspices of the Four Year Plan.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005361-87) In July 1937, the [Reichswerke Hermann Göring](/wiki/Reichswerke_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Reichswerke Hermann Göring") was established under state ownership – though led by Göring – with the aim of boosting steel production beyond the level which private enterprise could economically provide.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery2002145-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-17986%2C_Schorfheide%2C_Lord_Edward_Frederik_Halifax%2C_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_crop.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-17986%2C_Schorfheide%2C_Lord_Edward_Frederik_Halifax%2C_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-17986,_Schorfheide,_Lord_Edward_Frederik_Halifax,_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_crop.jpg)

Göring with British War Secretary [Lord Halifax](/wiki/Edward_Wood,_1st_Earl_of_Halifax "Edward Wood, 1st Earl of Halifax") at Schorfheide, 20 November 1937

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2004-1202-504%2C_Berlin%2C_Adolf_Hitler_und_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2004-1202-504%2C_Berlin%2C_Adolf_Hitler_und_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2004-1202-504,_Berlin,_Adolf_Hitler_und_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring.jpg)

Hitler with Göring on balcony of the Chancellery, Berlin, 16 March 1938

In 1938, Göring was involved in the [Blomberg–Fritsch Affair](/wiki/Blomberg%E2%80%93Fritsch_Affair "Blomberg–Fritsch Affair"), which led to the resignations of the War Minister, *[Generalfeldmarschall](/wiki/Generalfeldmarschall "Generalfeldmarschall")* [Werner von Blomberg](/wiki/Werner_von_Blomberg "Werner von Blomberg"), and the army commander, General [Werner von Fritsch](/wiki/Werner_von_Fritsch "Werner von Fritsch"). Göring had acted as witness at Blomberg's wedding to Margarethe Gruhn, a 26-year-old typist, on 12 January 1938. Information received from the police showed that the young bride was a prostitute.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerwarth2011116-89) Göring felt obligated to tell Hitler, but also saw this event as an opportunity to dispose of Blomberg. Blomberg was forced to resign. Göring did not want Fritsch to be appointed to that position and thus be his superior. Several days later, Heydrich revealed a file on Fritsch that contained allegations of homosexual activity and blackmail. The charges were later proven to be false, but Fritsch had lost Hitler's trust and was forced to resign.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerwarth2011116,_117-90) Hitler used the dismissals as an opportunity to reshuffle the leadership of the military. Göring asked for the post of War Minister but was turned down; he was appointed to the rank of *Generalfeldmarschall*. Hitler took over as [supreme commander of the armed forces](/wiki/Oberkommando_der_Wehrmacht "Oberkommando der Wehrmacht") and created subordinate posts to head the three main branches of service.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005642–644-91)

Main article: [Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss")

As minister in charge of the Four-Year Plan, Göring became concerned with the lack of natural resources in Germany and began pushing for Austria to be incorporated into the Reich. The province of [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria") had rich iron ore deposits, and the country as a whole was home to many skilled labourers who would also be useful. Hitler had always been in favour of a takeover of Austria, his native country. He met the Austrian Chancellor [Kurt Schuschnigg](/wiki/Kurt_Schuschnigg "Kurt Schuschnigg") on 12 February 1938, threatening invasion if peaceful unification was not forthcoming. The Nazi Party was made legal in Austria to gain a power base, and a referendum on reunification was scheduled for March. When Hitler did not approve of the wording of the plebiscite, Göring telephoned Schuschnigg and Austrian head of state [Wilhelm Miklas](/wiki/Wilhelm_Miklas "Wilhelm Miklas") to demand Schuschnigg's resignation, threatening invasion by German troops and civil unrest by the Austrian Nazi Party members. Schuschnigg resigned on 11 March and the plebiscite was cancelled. By 5:30 the next morning, German troops that had been massing on the border marched into Austria, meeting no resistance.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005646–652-92)

Main article: [German occupation of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Czechoslovakia "German occupation of Czechoslovakia")

Although [Joachim von Ribbentrop](/wiki/Joachim_von_Ribbentrop "Joachim von Ribbentrop") had been named Foreign Minister in February 1938, Göring continued to involve himself in foreign affairs.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011187-71) That July, he contacted the British government with the idea that he should make an official visit to discuss Germany's intentions for Czechoslovakia. [Neville Chamberlain](/wiki/Neville_Chamberlain "Neville Chamberlain") was in favour of a meeting and there was talk of a pact being signed between Britain and Germany. In February 1938, Göring visited Warsaw to quell rumours about the upcoming [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland"). He had conversations with the Hungarian government that summer as well, discussing their potential role in an invasion of Czechoslovakia. At the [Nuremberg Rally](/wiki/Nuremberg_Rally "Nuremberg Rally") that September, Göring and other speakers denounced the Czechs as an inferior race that must be conquered.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011194–197-93) Chamberlain and Hitler had a series of meetings that led to the signing of the [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement") (29 September 1938), which turned over control of the [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") to Germany.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005674-94) In March 1939, Göring threatened Czechoslovak president [Emil Hácha](/wiki/Emil_H%C3%A1cha "Emil Hácha") with the bombing of [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). Hácha then agreed to sign a communique accepting [the German occupation](/wiki/Occupation_of_Czechoslovakia_(1938%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Czechoslovakia (1938–1945)") of the remainder of [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") and [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENoakesPridham2001119-95)

Although many in the party disliked him,[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunther194019-96) before the war Göring enjoyed widespread personal popularity among the German public because of his perceived sociability, colour and humour.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery200273-97)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery2002236-98) As the Nazi leader most responsible for economic matters, he presented himself as a champion of national interests over allegedly corrupt big business and the old German elite. The Nazi press was on Göring's side. Other leaders, such as Hess and Ribbentrop, were envious of his popularity.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery200273-97) In Britain and the United States, some viewed Göring as more acceptable than the other Nazis and as a possible mediator between the western democracies and Hitler.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery2002236-98)

## World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_-_R%C3%B6hr.jpg/250px-Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_-_R%C3%B6hr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hermann_G%C3%B6ring_-_R%C3%B6hr.jpg)

Göring as *[Reichsmarschall](/wiki/Reichsmarschall "Reichsmarschall")*

### Success on all fronts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Success on all fronts")]

Göring and other senior officers were concerned that Germany was not yet ready for war, but Hitler insisted on pushing ahead as soon as possible.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011197,_211-99) On 30 August 1939, immediately prior to the outbreak of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), Hitler appointed Göring as the chairman of a new six-person [Council of Ministers for the Defense of the Reich](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_for_the_Defense_of_the_Reich "Council of Ministers for the Defense of the Reich") which was set up to operate as a war cabinet.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroszat1981308–309-100) The invasion of Poland, the opening action of World War II, began at dawn on 1 September 1939.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960597-101) Later in the day, speaking to the *Reichstag*, Hitler designated Göring as his successor as Führer of all Germany, "If anything should befall me",[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960599-102) with Hess as the second alternate.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunther194019-96) Major German victories followed one after the other in quick succession. With the help of the Luftwaffe, the [Polish Air Force](/wiki/Polish_Air_Force "Polish Air Force") was defeated within a week.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHooton1999177–189-103)[[d]](#cite_note-106) The *[Fallschirmjäger](/wiki/Fallschirmj%C3%A4ger "Fallschirmjäger")* seized vital airfields in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") ([Operation Weserübung](/wiki/Operation_Weser%C3%BCbung "Operation Weserübung")) and captured [Fort Eben-Emael](/wiki/Fort_Eben-Emael "Fort Eben-Emael") in Belgium on 10 May 1940, the first day of the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"). Göring's Luftwaffe played critical roles in the [Battles of the Netherlands](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Netherlands "Battle of the Netherlands"), [of Belgium](/wiki/Battle_of_Belgium "Battle of Belgium") and of France in May 1940.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960721,_723,_725-107)

After the [Fall of France](/wiki/Fall_of_France "Fall of France"), Hitler awarded Göring the [Grand Cross of the Iron Cross](/wiki/Grand_Cross_of_the_Iron_Cross "Grand Cross of the Iron Cross") for his successful leadership.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFellgiebel2000198-108) During the [1940 Field Marshal Ceremony](/wiki/1940_Field_Marshal_Ceremony "1940 Field Marshal Ceremony"), Hitler promoted Göring to the rank of *[Reichsmarschall](/wiki/Reichsmarschall "Reichsmarschall") des Grossdeutschen Reiches* (transl. Reich Marshal of the Greater German Reich), a specially created rank which made him senior to all field marshals in the military. As a result of this promotion, he was the highest-ranking soldier in Germany until the end of the war. Göring had already received the [Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross](/wiki/Knight%27s_Cross_of_the_Iron_Cross "Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross") on 30 September 1939 as Commander in Chief of the Luftwaffe.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFellgiebel2000198-108)

The UK had declared war on Germany on 3 September 1939, the third day of the invasion of Poland.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960615-109) In July 1940, Hitler began preparations for an invasion of Britain. As part of the plan, the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") (RAF) had to be neutralised. Bombing raids commenced on British air installations and on cities and centres of industry.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008113,_136,_143-110) Göring had by then already announced in a radio speech, "If as much as a single enemy aircraft flies over German soil, my name is Meier!",[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOestermann2001157-111) something that would return to haunt him, when the RAF began bombing German cities on 11 May 1940.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelwood2015-112) Though he was confident the Luftwaffe could defeat the RAF within days, Göring, like Admiral [Erich Raeder](/wiki/Erich_Raeder "Erich Raeder"), [commander-in-chief of the *Kriegsmarine*](/wiki/Oberkommando_der_Marine "Oberkommando der Marine") (navy),[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaeder2001324–325-113) was pessimistic about the chance of success of the planned invasion (codenamed [Operation Sea Lion](/wiki/Operation_Sea_Lion "Operation Sea Lion")).[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBungay2000337-114) Göring hoped that a victory in the air would be enough to force peace without an invasion. The campaign failed and Sea Lion was postponed indefinitely on 17 September 1940.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008144-115) After their defeat in the [Battle of Britain](/wiki/Battle_of_Britain "Battle of Britain"), the Luftwaffe attempted to defeat Britain via [strategic bombing](/wiki/Strategic_bombing "Strategic bombing"). On 12 October 1940 Hitler cancelled Sea Lion due to the onset of winter.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1965500-116) By the end of the year, it was clear that British morale was not being shaken by [the Blitz](/wiki/The_Blitz "The Blitz"), though the bombings continued through May 1941.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008145-117)

### Defeat on all fronts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Defeat on all fronts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Orders_issued_by_Hermann_Goring_for_German_troops.jpg/250px-Orders_issued_by_Hermann_Goring_for_German_troops.jpg)](/wiki/File:Orders_issued_by_Hermann_Goring_for_German_troops.jpg)

Göring with General der Flieger and Luftwaffe Chief of Staff [Hans Jeschonnek](/wiki/Hans_Jeschonnek "Hans Jeschonnek"), General der Flieger [Otto Hoffmann von Waldau](/wiki/Otto_Hoffmann_von_Waldau "Otto Hoffmann von Waldau") and General der Flieger [Gustav Kastner-Kirdorf](/wiki/Gustav_Kastner-Kirdorf "Gustav Kastner-Kirdorf") issuing an order for German troops on the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)"), 1941

In spite of the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"), signed in 1939, Nazi Germany began [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa")—the invasion of the Soviet Union—on 22 June 1941. Initially, the Luftwaffe was at an advantage, destroying thousands of Soviet aircraft in the first month of fighting.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008178–179-118) Hitler and his top staff were sure that the campaign would be over by Christmas, and no provisions were made for reserves of men or equipment.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008187-119) However, by July, the Germans had only 1,000 planes remaining in operation and their troop losses were over 213,000 men. The choice was made to concentrate the attack on only one part of the vast front; efforts would be directed at capturing Moscow.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008201-120) After the long, but successful, [Battle of Smolensk](/wiki/Battle_of_Smolensk_(1941) "Battle of Smolensk (1941)"), Hitler ordered [Army Group Centre](/wiki/Army_Group_Centre "Army Group Centre") to halt its advance to Moscow and temporarily diverted its Panzer groups north and south to aid in the encirclement of [Leningrad](/wiki/Leningrad "Leningrad") and [Kiev](/wiki/Kiev "Kiev").[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStolfi1982-121) The pause provided the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") with an opportunity to mobilise fresh reserves; historian [Russel Stolfi](/w/index.php?title=Russel_H._S._Stolfi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Russel H. S. Stolfi (page does not exist)") considers it to be one of the major factors that caused the failure of the Moscow offensive, which was resumed in October 1941 with the [Battle of Moscow](/wiki/Battle_of_Moscow "Battle of Moscow").[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStolfi1982-121) Poor weather conditions, fuel shortages, a delay in building aircraft bases in Eastern Europe and overstretched supply lines were also factors. Hitler did not give permission for even a partial retreat until mid-January 1942; by this time the losses were comparable to those of the [French invasion of Russia](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Russia "French invasion of Russia") in 1812.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008207–213-122)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Reprich-012-08%2C_Wolfschanze%2C_Hitler%2C_Ley%2C_Porsche_und_G%C3%B6ring.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Reprich-012-08%2C_Wolfschanze%2C_Hitler%2C_Ley%2C_Porsche_und_G%C3%B6ring.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Reprich-012-08,_Wolfschanze,_Hitler,_Ley,_Porsche_und_G%C3%B6ring.jpg)

Hitler, Dr [Robert Ley](/wiki/Robert_Ley "Robert Ley"), automotive engineer [Ferdinand Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Porsche "Ferdinand Porsche") and Göring at the *[Wolf's Lair](/wiki/Wolf%27s_Lair "Wolf's Lair")* in 1942

In late October or early November 1941, Hitler and Göring decided on the mass deportation of [Soviet prisoners of war](/wiki/German_atrocities_committed_against_Soviet_prisoners_of_war "German atrocities committed against Soviet prisoners of war")—and a larger number of Soviet civilians—to Germany for [forced labor](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II"), but epidemics soon caused the halting of prisoner-of-war transports.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeller2021204-123)[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGerlach2016228-124) Those who were deported to Germany faced conditions not necessarily any better than existed in the [occupied Soviet Union](/wiki/German-occupied_Europe "German-occupied Europe").[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPohl2012214-125) By the end of the war, at least 1.3 million Soviet prisoners of war had been deported to Germany or its annexed territories. Of these, 400,000 did not survive and most of these [deaths](/wiki/World_War_II_casualties_of_the_Soviet_Union "World War II casualties of the Soviet Union") occurred in the winter of 1941/1942.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPohl2012215-126)

After the [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor"), Göring, along with Field Marshal [Wilhelm Keitel](/wiki/Wilhelm_Keitel "Wilhelm Keitel") and Admiral [Erich Raeder](/wiki/Erich_Raeder "Erich Raeder"), urged Hitler to immediately declare war on the United States.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFleming1987-127)

Hitler decided that the summer 1942 campaign would be concentrated in the south; efforts would be made to capture the oilfields in the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus").[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008404–405-128) The [Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad"), a major turning point of the war,[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008421-129) began on 23 August 1942 with a bombing campaign by the Luftwaffe.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008409-130) The German [Sixth Army](/wiki/6th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "6th Army (Wehrmacht)") entered the city, but because of its location on the front line, it was still possible for the Soviets to encircle and trap it there without reinforcements or supplies. When the Sixth Army was surrounded by the end of November in [Operation Uranus](/wiki/Operation_Uranus "Operation Uranus"), Göring promised that the Luftwaffe would be able to deliver a minimum of 300 tons of supplies to the trapped men every day. On the basis of these assurances, Hitler demanded that there be no retreat; they were to fight to the last man. Though some airlifts were able to get through, supplies delivered never exceeded 120 tons per day.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008412–413-131)[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1971329-132) The remnants of the Sixth Army—some 91,000 men out of an army of 285,000—surrendered in early February 1943; only 5,000 of these captives survived the [Soviet prisoner of war camps](/wiki/German_prisoners_of_war_in_the_Soviet_Union "German prisoners of war in the Soviet Union") to see Germany again.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1960932-133)

### War over Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: War over Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1977-149-13%2C_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring%2C_Adolf_Hitler%2C_Albert_Speer.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1977-149-13%2C_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring%2C_Adolf_Hitler%2C_Albert_Speer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1977-149-13,_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring,_Adolf_Hitler,_Albert_Speer.jpg)

Göring with Hitler and [Albert Speer](/wiki/Albert_Speer "Albert Speer"), 10 August 1943

Meanwhile, the strength of the US and British bomber fleets had increased. Based in Britain, they began [operations against German targets](/wiki/Defence_of_the_Reich "Defence of the Reich"). The first thousand-bomber raid was staged [on Cologne](/wiki/Bombing_of_Cologne_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Cologne in World War II") on 30 May 1942.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008438,_441-134) Air raids continued on targets farther from England after auxiliary fuel tanks were installed on US [fighter aircraft](/wiki/Fighter_aircraft "Fighter aircraft"). Göring refused to believe reports that American fighters had been shot down as far east as [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen") in winter 1942–1943. His reputation began to decline.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1971378-135)

The American [P-51 Mustang](/wiki/P-51_Mustang "P-51 Mustang"), with a [combat radius](/wiki/Combat_radius "Combat radius") of over 1,800 miles (2,900 km) when using underwing [drop tanks](/wiki/Drop_tank "Drop tank"), began to escort the bombers in large formations to and from the target area in early 1944. From that point onwards, the Luftwaffe began to suffer casualties in aircrews it could not sufficiently replace. By targeting oil refineries and rail communications, [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") bombers crippled the German war effort by late 1944.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008461-136) German civilians blamed Göring for his failure to protect the homeland.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008447-137) Hitler began excluding him from conferences but retained him in his positions at the head of the Luftwaffe and as plenipotentiary of the Four-Year Plan.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011296,_297,_299-138) As he lost Hitler's trust, Göring began to spend more time at his various residences.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008510-139) On [D-Day](/wiki/D-Day "D-Day") (6 June 1944), the Luftwaffe only had some 300 fighters and a small number of bombers in the area of the landings; the Allies had a total strength of 11,000 aircraft.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011295,_302-140)

### End of the war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: End of the war")]

See also: [Göring telegram](/wiki/G%C3%B6ring_telegram "Göring telegram")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Goeringcaptivity.jpg/250px-Goeringcaptivity.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goeringcaptivity.jpg)

Göring in captivity 9 May 1945

As the [Soviets approached Berlin](/wiki/Battle_of_Berlin "Battle of Berlin"), Hitler's efforts to organise the defence of the city became ever more meaningless and futile.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008725-141) His last birthday, celebrated at the *[Führerbunker](/wiki/F%C3%BChrerbunker "Führerbunker")* in Berlin on 20 April 1945, was the occasion for leave-taking by many top Nazis, Göring included. By this time, Göring's hunting lodge [Carinhall](/wiki/Carinhall "Carinhall") had been evacuated, the building destroyed,[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011310-142) and its art treasures moved to [Berchtesgaden](/wiki/Berchtesgaden "Berchtesgaden") and elsewhere.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008722-143) Göring arrived at his estate at Obersalzberg on 22 April, the same day that Hitler, in a lengthy diatribe against his generals, first publicly admitted that the war was lost and that he intended to remain in Berlin to the end and then commit suicide.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008723-144) Göring also stated that he was in a better position to negotiate a peace settlement.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer19601115–1116-145)

[OKW](/wiki/Oberkommando_der_Wehrmacht "Oberkommando der Wehrmacht") operations chief [Alfred Jodl](/wiki/Alfred_Jodl "Alfred Jodl") was present for Hitler's rant and notified Göring's chief of staff, [Karl Koller](/wiki/Karl_Koller_(general) "Karl Koller (general)"), at a meeting a few hours later. Sensing its implications, Koller immediately flew to Berchtesgaden to notify Göring of this development. A week after the start of the Soviet invasion, Hitler had issued a decree naming Göring his successor in the event of his death, thus codifying the declaration he had made soon after the beginning of the war. The decree also gave Göring full authority to act as Hitler's deputy if Hitler ever lost his freedom of action.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer19601115–1116-145)

Göring feared being branded a traitor if he tried to take power, but also feared being accused of dereliction of duty if he did nothing. After some hesitation, Göring reviewed his copy of the 1941 decree naming him Hitler's successor. After conferring with Koller and [Hans Lammers](/wiki/Hans_Lammers "Hans Lammers") (the state secretary of the Reich Chancellery), Göring concluded that by remaining in Berlin to face certain death, Hitler had incapacitated himself from governing. All agreed that under the terms of the decree, it was incumbent upon Göring to take power in Hitler's stead.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer19601116-146) He was also motivated by fears that his rival, [Martin Bormann](/wiki/Martin_Bormann "Martin Bormann"), would seize power upon Hitler's death and would have him killed as a traitor. With this in mind, Göring sent a carefully worded telegram asking Hitler for permission to take over as the leader of Germany, stressing that he would be acting as Hitler's deputy. He added that, if Hitler did not reply by 22:00 that night (23 April), he would assume that Hitler had indeed lost his freedom of action and would assume leadership of the Reich.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011315-147)

Göring after his capture (15 May 1945)

The telegram was intercepted by Bormann, who convinced Hitler that Göring was attempting a coup. Bormann argued that Göring's telegram was not a request for permission to act as Hitler's deputy, but a demand to resign or be overthrown.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer19601118-148) Bormann also intercepted another telegram in which Göring directed Ribbentrop to report to him if there was no further communication from Hitler or Göring before midnight.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1971608–609-149) Hitler sent a reply to Göring—prepared with Bormann's help—rescinding the 1941 decree and threatening him with execution for high treason unless he immediately resigned from all of his offices. Realising his situation was untenable, Göring duly resigned. Afterwards, Hitler (or Bormann, depending on the source) ordered the SS to place Göring, his staff and Lammers under house arrest at Obersalzberg.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer19601118-148)[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008724-150) Bormann made an announcement over the radio that Göring had resigned for health reasons.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011318-151)

By 26 April, the complex at Obersalzberg [was under attack](/wiki/Bombing_of_Obersalzberg "Bombing of Obersalzberg") by the Allies, so Göring was moved to [his castle at Mauterndorf](/wiki/Burg_Mauterndorf "Burg Mauterndorf"). In his [last will and testament](/wiki/Last_will_and_testament_of_Adolf_Hitler "Last will and testament of Adolf Hitler"), Hitler expelled Göring from the party, formally rescinded the decree making him his successor and upbraided Göring for "illegally attempting to seize control of the state".[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer19601126-152) He then appointed [Karl Dönitz](/wiki/Karl_D%C3%B6nitz "Karl Dönitz"), the Navy's commander-in-chief, as president of the Reich and supreme commander of the [armed forces](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht"). Hitler and his wife, [Eva Braun](/wiki/Eva_Braun "Eva Braun"), [committed suicide](/wiki/Death_of_Adolf_Hitler "Death of Adolf Hitler") on 30 April 1945, a few hours after a hastily arranged wedding. Göring was freed on 5 May by a passing Luftwaffe unit and made his way to the U.S. lines in hopes of surrendering to them rather than to the Soviets. He was taken into custody near [Radstadt](/wiki/Radstadt "Radstadt") on 9 May by elements of the [36th Infantry Division](/wiki/36th_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "36th Infantry Division (United States)") of the [US Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army").[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011320–325-153)[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2008728-154)[[e]](#cite_note-156) This move likely saved Göring's life; Bormann had ordered him executed if Berlin had fallen.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer19601128-157) On 10 May, US Air Forces commander [Carl Spaatz](/wiki/Carl_Spaatz "Carl Spaatz"), along with Lieutenant General [Hoyt Vandenberg](/wiki/Hoyt_Vandenberg "Hoyt Vandenberg") and Spaatz's [special consultant](/wiki/Private_secretary "Private secretary") [Bruce Campbell Hopper](/wiki/Bruce_Campbell_Hopper "Bruce Campbell Hopper"), conducted an interrogation of Göring at the Ritter School in [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg"), Germany.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSAF1945-158)

## Trial and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Trial and death")]

Main article: [Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Defendants_in_the_dock_at_the_Nuremberg_Trials.jpg/250px-Defendants_in_the_dock_at_the_Nuremberg_Trials.jpg)](/wiki/File:Defendants_in_the_dock_at_the_Nuremberg_Trials.jpg)

Göring (first row, far left) at the Nuremberg trial

Göring was flown to [Camp Ashcan](/wiki/Camp_Ashcan "Camp Ashcan"), a temporary prisoner-of-war camp housed in the Palace Hotel at [Mondorf-les-Bains](/wiki/Mondorf-les-Bains "Mondorf-les-Bains"), Luxembourg. Here he was weaned off [dihydrocodeine](/wiki/Dihydrocodeine "Dihydrocodeine") (a mild morphine derivative)—he had been taking the equivalent of three or four grains (260 to 320 mg) of morphine a day—and was put on a strict diet; he lost 60 pounds (27 kg). His [IQ](/wiki/Intelligence_quotient "Intelligence quotient") was tested while in custody and found to be 138.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert199531-159) Top Nazi officials were transferred in September to Nuremberg, which was to be the location of a series of military tribunals beginning in November.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011329–331-160) At Nuremberg, Göring was examined by Chief Medical Officer Lt. Col. [Rene Juchli](/wiki/Rene_Juchli "Rene Juchli"), who reported that Göring was "still suffering from heart disease and excess fat, both due to glandular disturbance."[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVallejo_Times-HeraldOctober_1945-161)

Göring was the second highest-ranking official tried at Nuremberg, behind Reich President (former Admiral) Karl Dönitz. The prosecution levelled an indictment of four charges, including a charge of conspiracy; waging a war of aggression; war crimes, including the [plundering and removal to Germany of works of art and other property](/wiki/Nazi_plunder "Nazi plunder"); and crimes against humanity, including the disappearance of political and other opponents under the *[Nacht und Nebel](/wiki/Nacht_und_Nebel "Nacht und Nebel")* (transl. Night and Fog) decree; the torture and ill treatment of prisoners of war; and the murder and enslavement of civilians, including what was at the time estimated to be 5,700,000 Jews. When asked for his plea, Göring attempted to read a lengthy statement to the court, but was rebuked by presiding judge [Sir Geoffrey Lawrence](/wiki/Geoffrey_Lawrence,_1st_Baron_Oaksey "Geoffrey Lawrence, 1st Baron Oaksey") and instructed to simply plead either "guilty" or "not guilty";[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPathé1945-162) Göring then declared himself to be "in the sense of the indictment, not guilty".[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011336–337-163)

The trial lasted 218 days. The prosecution presented its case from November to March, and Göring's defence—the first to be presented—lasted from 8 to 22 March. The sentences were read on 30 September 1946.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011337-164) Göring, forced to remain silent while seated in the dock, communicated his opinions about the proceedings using gestures, shaking his head, or laughing. He constantly took notes, whispered with the other defendants and tried to control the erratic behaviour of Hess, who was seated beside him.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011339-165) During breaks in the proceedings, Göring tried to dominate the other defendants, and he was eventually placed in solitary confinement when he attempted to influence their testimony.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011341–342-166) Göring told American psychiatrist [Leon Goldensohn](/wiki/Leon_Goldensohn "Leon Goldensohn") that the court was "stupid" to try "little fellows" like Funk and [Kaltenbrunner](/wiki/Ernst_Kaltenbrunner "Ernst Kaltenbrunner") instead of letting Göring take all the blame on himself.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldensohn2004-167) He also claimed that he had never heard of most of the other defendants before the trial.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldensohn2004-167)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Goering_on_trial_%28color%29.jpg/250px-Goering_on_trial_%28color%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goering_on_trial_(color).jpg)

Göring at the Nuremberg trials

On several occasions over the course of the trial, the prosecution showed films of the [concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps") and other atrocities. Everyone present, including Göring, found the contents of the films shocking; he said that the films must have been faked. Witnesses, including [Paul Körner](/wiki/Paul_K%C3%B6rner_(Nazi_official) "Paul Körner (Nazi official)") and [Erhard Milch](/wiki/Erhard_Milch "Erhard Milch"), tried to portray Göring as a peaceful moderate. Milch stated that it had been impossible to oppose Hitler or disobey his orders; to do so would likely have meant death for oneself and one's family.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011343–347-168) When testifying on his own behalf, Göring emphasised his loyalty to Hitler and claimed to know nothing about what had happened in the concentration camps, which were under Himmler's control. He provided evasive, convoluted answers to direct questions and had plausible excuses for all of his actions during the war. He used the witness stand as a venue to expound at great length on his own role in the Reich, attempting to present himself as a peacemaker and diplomat before the outbreak of the war.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011359–367-169) During cross-examination, chief prosecutor [Robert H. Jackson](/wiki/Robert_H._Jackson "Robert H. Jackson") read the minutes of a meeting that had been held shortly after [Kristallnacht](/wiki/Kristallnacht "Kristallnacht"), a major [pogrom](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom") in November 1938. At the meeting, Göring had plotted to confiscate Jewish property in the wake of the pogrom.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011369-170) Later, [David Maxwell-Fyfe](/wiki/David_Maxwell_Fyfe,_1st_Earl_of_Kilmuir "David Maxwell Fyfe, 1st Earl of Kilmuir") presented evidence that Göring must have known about [the killing of 50 airmen](/wiki/Stalag_Luft_III_murders "Stalag Luft III murders") who had been recaptured after escaping from [Stalag Luft III](/wiki/Stalag_Luft_III "Stalag Luft III") and could have saved them.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011371-171) He also presented evidence that Göring knew about the extermination of the [Hungarian Jews](/wiki/Hungarian_Jews "Hungarian Jews").[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011374–375-172)

Göring was found guilty on all four counts and was sentenced to death by hanging. The judgment stated:

> There is nothing to be said in mitigation. For Göring was often, indeed almost always, the moving force, second only to his leader. He was the leading war aggressor, both as political and as military leader; he was the director of the slave labour programme and the creator of the oppressive programme against the Jews and other races, at home and abroad. All of these crimes he has frankly admitted. On some specific cases there may be conflict of testimony, but in terms of the broad outline, his own admissions are more than sufficiently wide to be conclusive of his guilt. His guilt is unique in its enormity. The record discloses no excuses for this man.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Military_Tribunal1946-173)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Goering-corpse.jpg/250px-Goering-corpse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goering-corpse.jpg)

Göring's corpse

Göring made an appeal asking to be shot as a soldier instead of hanged as a common criminal, but the court refused.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011392–393-174)

The night before he was to be hanged, however, Göring committed suicide with a [potassium cyanide](/wiki/Potassium_cyanide "Potassium cyanide") capsule.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2008964-175) Since he was guarded around the clock, it was not clear how Göring obtained the poison. At the time, speculation centered on whether he had access to a jar of his hair cream where another cyanide capsule was later found. Decades later, another theory focused on a U.S. Army lieutenant, Jack G. Wheelis, who had been stationed at the trials but who had since died. Göring had given Wheelis his gold watch, pen and cigarette case,[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBotting2006280-176) and so it was theorised that Wheelis had retrieved the capsules from their hiding place among Göring's confiscated personal effects and then passed them along.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1992623-177)

In 2005, former U.S. Army private Herbert Lee Stivers, who served in the [1st Infantry Division](/wiki/1st_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "1st Infantry Division (United States)")'s [26th Infantry Regiment](/wiki/26th_Infantry_Regiment "26th Infantry Regiment")—the honour guard for the Nuremberg Trials—claimed he gave Göring "medicine" hidden inside a fountain pen that a young German woman, Mona, had asked him to smuggle into the prison. In explaining his decision to speak out 60 years later, Stivers said that he was convinced to come forward by his daughter. He wanted to set the record straight: he thought that he was just offering medicine; he said that he did not know what was in the pill until after Göring's suicide.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC_News2005-178)

Göring's body, as with those of the ten men who [were executed](/wiki/Nuremberg_executions "Nuremberg executions"), was displayed at the execution ground for witnesses. The bodies were cremated at [Ostfriedhof](/wiki/Ostfriedhof_(Munich) "Ostfriedhof (Munich)"), Munich, and the ashes scattered in the [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") River.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDarnstädt2005-179)[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011393-180)[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery2001205-181)

## Personal properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Personal properties")]

See also: [Nazi plunder](/wiki/Nazi_plunder "Nazi plunder") and [Reichswerke Hermann Göring](/wiki/Reichswerke_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Reichswerke Hermann Göring")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/G%C3%B6ring_weapon_and_baton.jpg/250px-G%C3%B6ring_weapon_and_baton.jpg)](/wiki/File:G%C3%B6ring_weapon_and_baton.jpg)

Göring's *Reichsmarschall* baton and [Smith & Wesson Model 10](/wiki/Smith_%26_Wesson_Model_10 "Smith & Wesson Model 10") revolver. To the left is the silver-bound guest book from [Carinhall](/wiki/Carinhall "Carinhall") ([West Point Museum](/wiki/West_Point_Museum "West Point Museum")).

Göring's name is closely associated with the Nazi plunder of Jewish property. His name appears 135 times on the [OSS Art Looting Investigation Unit (ALIU) Red Flag Names List](/wiki/Nazi_plunder#Art_Looting_Investigation_Unit "Nazi plunder")[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOSS_Reports-182) compiled by US Army intelligence in 1945–46 and declassified in 1997.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENARA_Records-183)

The confiscation of Jewish property gave Göring the opportunity to amass a personal fortune. Some properties he seized himself or acquired for a nominal price. In other cases, he collected bribes for allowing others to steal Jewish property. He took [kickbacks](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption") from industrialists for favourable decisions as Four-Year Plan director and money for supplying arms to the Spanish Republicans in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Foreign_involvement_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Foreign involvement in the Spanish Civil War") via [Pyrkal](/wiki/Pyrkal "Pyrkal") in Greece (although Germany was supporting Franco and the Nationalists).[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006366–368,_538-184)

Göring was appointed Reich Master of the Hunt in 1933 and Master of the German Forests in 1934. He instituted reforms to the forestry laws and acted to protect endangered species. Around this time, he became interested in [Schorfheide Forest](/wiki/Schorfheide-Chorin_Biosphere_Reserve "Schorfheide-Chorin Biosphere Reserve"), where he set aside 100,000 acres (400 km2) as a state park, which is still extant. There he built an elaborate hunting lodge, Carinhall, in memory of his first wife, Carin. By 1934, her body had been transported to the site and placed in a vault on the estate.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011120–123-185) Through most of the 1930s, Göring kept pet lion cubs, borrowed from the [Berlin Zoo](/wiki/Berlin_Zoological_Garden "Berlin Zoological Garden"), both at Carinhall and at his house at [Obersalzberg](/wiki/Obersalzberg "Obersalzberg").[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKellerhoff2018-186) The main lodge at Carinhall had a large art gallery where Göring displayed works that had been plundered from private collections and museums around Europe from 1939 onward.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1971244–245-187)[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERothfeld2002-188) Göring worked closely with the *Einsatzstab Reichsleiter Rosenberg* (transl. [Reichsleiter Rosenberg Taskforce](/wiki/Reichsleiter_Rosenberg_Taskforce "Reichsleiter Rosenberg Taskforce")), an organisation tasked with the looting of artwork and cultural material from Jewish collections, libraries and museums throughout Europe.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011283–285-189) Headed by Alfred Rosenberg, the task force set up a collection centre and headquarters in Paris. Some 26,000 railroad cars full of art treasures, furniture and other looted items were sent to Germany from France alone. Göring repeatedly visited the Paris headquarters to review the incoming stolen goods and to select items to be sent on a special train to Carinhall and his other homes.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011283–285,_291-190) The estimated value of his collection, which numbered some 1,500 pieces, was $200 million.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011281-191)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Standarte_des_Reichsmarschalls_%281941%E2%80%9345%29.jpg/250px-Standarte_des_Reichsmarschalls_%281941%E2%80%9345%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Standarte_des_Reichsmarschalls_(1941%E2%80%9345).jpg)

Standard, on display at the *Musée de la Guerre* in [Les Invalides](/wiki/Les_Invalides "Les Invalides"), Paris

Göring was known for his extravagant tastes and garish clothing. He had various special uniforms made for the many posts he held;[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011115–116-192) his *Reichsmarschall* uniform included a jewel-encrusted baton. [Hans-Ulrich Rudel](/wiki/Hans-Ulrich_Rudel "Hans-Ulrich Rudel"), the top *[Stuka](/wiki/Stuka "Stuka")* pilot of the war, recalled twice meeting Göring dressed in outlandish costumes: first, a medieval hunting costume, practicing archery with his doctor; and second, dressed in a red [toga](/wiki/Toga "Toga") fastened with a golden clasp, smoking an unusually large pipe. [Italian Foreign Minister](/wiki/Italian_Minister_of_Foreign_Affairs "Italian Minister of Foreign Affairs") [Galeazzo Ciano](/wiki/Galeazzo_Ciano "Galeazzo Ciano") once noted Göring wearing a fur coat that looked like what "a high-grade prostitute wears to the opera".[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFussell200224–25-193) He threw lavish housewarming parties each time a round of construction was completed at Carinhall and changed costumes several times throughout the evenings.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011122-194)

Göring was noted for his patronage of music, especially opera. He entertained frequently and sumptuously and hosted elaborate birthday parties for himself.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1971417-195) Armaments minister [Albert Speer](/wiki/Albert_Speer "Albert Speer") recalled that guests brought expensive gifts, such as gold bars, Dutch cigars and valuable artwork. For his birthday in 1944, Speer gave Göring an oversized marble bust of Hitler.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1971416–417-196) As a member of the Prussian Council of State, Speer was required to donate a considerable portion of his salary towards the council's birthday gift to Göring without even being asked. *Generalfeldmarschall* Milch told Speer that similar donations were required out of the Air Ministry's general fund.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1971417–418-197) For his birthday in 1940, Ciano decorated Göring with the coveted [Collar of Annunziata](/wiki/Supreme_Order_of_the_Most_Holy_Annunciation "Supreme Order of the Most Holy Annunciation"). The award reduced him to tears.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMosley1974280-198)

The design of the *Reichsmarschall* standard, on a light blue field, featured a gold [German eagle](/wiki/German_eagle "German eagle") grasping a wreath surmounted by two batons overlaid with a swastika. The reverse side of the flag had the *Großkreuz des Eisernen Kreuzes* (transl. Grand Cross of the Iron Cross) surrounded by a wreath between four Luftwaffe eagles. The flag was carried by a personal standard-bearer at all public occasions.

Though he liked to be called "*der Eiserne*" (transl. the Iron Man), the former fighter pilot had become corpulent. He was one of the few Nazi leaders who did not take offence at hearing jokes about himself, "no matter how rude", taking them as a sign of his popularity among the masses. One such German joke poked fun at Göring by stating that he would wear an admiral's uniform with rubber medals to take a bath. His obesity was also a target, it being joked that "he sits down on his stomach".[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlockTrow1971330-199)[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunther194065-200) Another joke claimed that he had sent a wire to Hitler after his visit to the Vatican: "Mission accomplished. Pope unfrocked. Tiara and pontifical vestments are a perfect fit."[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2005409-201)

## Role in the Holocaust

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Role in the Holocaust")]

See also: [Luftwaffe § War crimes](/wiki/Luftwaffe#War_crimes "Luftwaffe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Carta_G%C3%B6ring.JPG/250px-Carta_G%C3%B6ring.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carta_G%C3%B6ring.JPG)

Göring's July 1941 letter to [Reinhard Heydrich](/wiki/Reinhard_Heydrich "Reinhard Heydrich")

[Joseph Goebbels](/wiki/Joseph_Goebbels "Joseph Goebbels") and Himmler were far more antisemitic than Göring, who mainly adopted that attitude because party politics required him to do so.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011136–137-202) His deputy [Erhard Milch](/wiki/Erhard_Milch "Erhard Milch") had a Jewish parent. However, Göring supported the [Nuremberg Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws") of 1935 and later initiated economic measures unfavourable to Jews.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011136–137-202) He required the registration of all Jewish property as part of the Four-Year Plan, and at a meeting held after *Kristallnacht* was livid that the financial burden for the Jewish losses would have to be made good by German-owned insurance companies. He proposed that the Jews be fined one billion [marks](/wiki/Reichsmark "Reichsmark").[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011189–191-203)

At the same meeting, options for the disposition of the Jews and their property were discussed. Jews would be segregated into ghettos or encouraged to emigrate, and their property would be seized in a programme of [Aryanisation](/wiki/Aryanization_(Nazism) "Aryanization (Nazism)"). Compensation for seized property would be low, if any was given at all.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011189–191-203) Detailed minutes of this meeting and other documents were read out at the Nuremberg trial, proving his knowledge of and complicity with the persecution of the Jews.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011369-170)

On 24 January 1939, Göring established in Berlin the head office of the [Central Office for Jewish Emigration](/wiki/Central_Office_for_Jewish_Emigration "Central Office for Jewish Emigration"),[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilberg1985160-204) modelled on the similar organisation established in Vienna in August 1938.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECesarani200562-205) Under the direction of Heydrich, it was tasked with using any means necessary to prompt Jews to leave the Reich, and with creating a Jewish organisation that would co-ordinate emigration from the Jewish side.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECesarani200577-206)

In July 1941, Göring issued a memo to Heydrich ordering him to organise the practical details of the [Final Solution](/wiki/Final_Solution "Final Solution") to the "Jewish Question". By the time that this letter was written, many Jews and others had already been killed in Poland, [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") and elsewhere. At the [Wannsee Conference](/wiki/Wannsee_Conference "Wannsee Conference"), held six months later, Heydrich formally announced that genocide of the Jews was now official Reich policy. Göring did not attend the conference, but he was present at other meetings where the number of people killed was discussed.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011259–260-207)[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood200175-208)

Göring directed [anti-partisan](/wiki/Anti-partisan "Anti-partisan") operations by Luftwaffe security battalions in the [Białowieża Forest](/wiki/Bia%C5%82owie%C5%BCa_Forest "Białowieża Forest") between 1942 and 1944 that resulted in the murder of thousands of Jews and Polish civilians.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlood2010261–262,_266-209)

At the Nuremberg trial, Göring told [first lieutenant](/wiki/First_lieutenant "First lieutenant") and U.S. Army psychologist [Gustave Gilbert](/wiki/Gustave_Gilbert "Gustave Gilbert") that he would never have supported the anti-Jewish measures if he had known what was going to happen. "I only thought we would eliminate Jews from positions in big business and government", he claimed.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManvellFraenkel2011378-210)[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilbert1995208-211)

### German

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: German")]

* [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"):
  + [Iron Cross](/wiki/Iron_Cross "Iron Cross") 2nd Class (15 September 1914)[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)
  + Iron Cross 1st Class (22 March 1915)[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)
  + Royal [House Order of Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_Order_of_Hohenzollern "House Order of Hohenzollern"), Knights Cross with Swords[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201589-213)
  + Order *[Pour le Mérite](/wiki/Pour_le_M%C3%A9rite "Pour le Mérite")* (2 June 1918)[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)
* [Grand Duchy of Baden](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden "Grand Duchy of Baden"):
  + [Military Karl-Friedrich Merit Order](/wiki/Military_Karl-Friedrich_Merit_Order "Military Karl-Friedrich Merit Order"), Knights Cross[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201589-213)
  + [Order of the Zähringer Lion](/wiki/Order_of_the_Z%C3%A4hringer_Lion "Order of the Zähringer Lion"), Knights Cross 2nd Class with Swords[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201589-213)
* [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"):
  + 1939 [Clasp to the Iron Cross](/wiki/Clasp_to_the_Iron_Cross "Clasp to the Iron Cross") 2nd Class (30 September 1939)[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)
  + 1939 Clasp to the Iron Cross 1st Class (30 September 1939)[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)
  + [Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross](/wiki/Knight%27s_Cross_of_the_Iron_Cross "Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross") (30 September 1939)[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)
  + Grand Cross of the Iron Cross for "the victories of the Luftwaffe in 1940 during the French campaign" (the only award of this decoration during World War II – 19 August 1940)[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillerSchulz201589-213)
  + [Golden Party Badge](/wiki/Golden_Party_Badge "Golden Party Badge")[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)
  + [Blood Order](/wiki/Blood_Order "Blood Order") (Commemorative Medal of 9 November 1923)[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)
  + [Danzig Cross](/wiki/Danzig_Cross "Danzig Cross"), 1st and 2nd class[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller2006442-212)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hermann_G%C3%B6ring&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-spelling_4-0)** *Göring* is the German spelling, but the name is [commonly transliterated](/wiki/English_terms_with_diacritical_marks#Words_imported_from_other_languages "English terms with diacritical marks") as *Goering* in English and other languages, using ⟨[oe](/wiki/Oe_(digraph) "Oe (digraph)")⟩, an alternative German spelling for ö (o with an [umlaut](/wiki/Umlaut_(diacritic) "Umlaut (diacritic)")) in general.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-swastika_26-0)** The swastika was a badge that the count and some friends had adopted at school, and he adopted it as a family emblem. See [Manvell & Fraenkel 2011](#CITEREFManvellFraenkel2011), pp. 403–404.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-56)** By 1930, the Nazi party claimed upwards of 293,000 members.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilders2017131-55)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-106)** Confident that the Luftwaffe was without peer and practically invincible in the wake of these victories, Göring commented to the German press that should the enemy ever penetrate German airspace, they could call him "Meyer".[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoorhouse2012350-104)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry201345fn-105)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-156)** Upon being captured by American soldiers, Göring immediately asked to be taken before Eisenhower. He hoped to be treated as a "spokesman for Germany".[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOvery2012228-155)
